macro_segments.title: The Mixing of Bull and Goat Blood on the Horns of the Golden Altar

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the day's topic: Combining bull and goat blood for the golden altar
Comparison to the sin-offering of the community (par he'elam davar) and distinctions in sprinklings
Rav Yoshiya's principle that vav in Torah implies combination unless otherwise indicated
Baraita debate: Rav Yoshiya vs. Rav Yonasan on whether bull and goat blood are mixed at the horns
Rav Yoshiya's response citing 'achat' (once) to indicate one combined atonement
Rav Yonasan's counter-argument that 'midam hapar umidam hase'ir' indicates separate applications

macro_segments.title: Technical Issues of Mixture and the Practical Procedure of Pouring Blood

micro_segments.title: The procedure of pouring the full bowl into the empty bowl to achieve proper mixing
Rami bar Chama's question on whether one vessel within another constitutes a chatzitzah (interposition)
Talmudic discussion of min b'mino (same type within same type) and whether it blocks connection
Proof from the Mishnah that vessels within vessels do not constitute a chatzitzah for service
Gemara's distinction that a foot is different because it cannot be annulled, unlike vessels
Extended discussion of the concept of chatzitzah: foreign body versus intentionality (makpid)
Question of whether derech sheirud (manner of service) is problematic with nested vessels
Baraita from Rabbi Yishmael: two vessels count as one service (shnei kelim, sheirus echad)
Question on sieve (siv) placed in bowl: does blood seeping through over time invalidate chatzitzah status
Distinction between porous materials for liquids versus solids regarding chatzitzah
Tosafos on lerchetz vs. chatzitzah distinctions between lulav (indirect taking) and kli sharet (direct contact)

macro_segments.title: The Exit to the Outer Altar and Application of Blood on Its Horns

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: descent motion (yored) of finger when placing blood, beginning at northeast corner
Rabbi Eliezer's position: no need to walk around altar due to its small size (amah al amah)
Application direction: bottom-to-top on distant corners, top-to-bottom on nearest corner
Rashi's explanation of why the nearest corner requires top-to-bottom motion to prevent blood from running down
Sprinkling seven times on the purity (taharo) of the altar
Pouring remainder blood at the base: western base for outer altar, southern base for inner
Mixing of all bloods through floor channels to Kidron Valley and sale to gardeners for fertilizer

macro_segments.title: Scriptural Interpretation of 'Vayatzah El HaMizbe'ach' and Location of Kohen During Service

micro_segments.title: Baraita: 'Vayatzah' indicates kohen must move from inside (between altar and parochet) to outside
Comparison to sin-offering of community (par ha'elam davar): kohen stands outside and sprinkles toward parochet
Second baraita: 'asher lifnei Hashem' modifies mizbe'ach (not kohen); kohen stands outside
Distinction between Yom Kippur service (seven on altar inside, then outside for remainder) and regular service
Talmudic clarification: 'vayatzah' occurs after sprinkling on parochet, not during

macro_segments.title: Starting Corner for Application: Debate Between Rabbi Akiva and Rabbi Yose HaGalili

micro_segments.title: Talmudic teaching on which corner to begin: southeast to northwest (clockwise) per Rabbi Akiva
Rabbi Yose HaGalili's alternative: northeast to southeast (counterclockwise)
Consensus that neither begins at the first corner encountered upon exit (western side)
Proof from 'vayatzah': kohen must fully exit the altar before beginning application
Exploration of whether counterclockwise rule (from Solomon's sea) applies inside the Heichal
Principle of 'ein ma'avirin al hamitzvot': return to missed corner obligation
Explanation of directional difference: Rabbi Akiva's clockwise due to returning to first missed corner

topical_tags.term: Mixing bloods (dam hapar and dam hase'ir)
Rav Yoshiya's principle on vav (conjunction) interpretation
Min b'mino (same type within same type)
Vayatzah el hamizbe'ach (exit to the altar)
Lifnei Hashem (before God/directed toward inner sanctum)
Ein ma'avirin al hamitzvot (do not pass by opportunity to perform mitzvah)
Rabbi Eliezer vs. other opinions on positioning during service
Rabbi Akiva vs. Rabbi Yose HaGalili on starting corner and direction
